questions.specification:
Analyze the following diagram of a pond ecosystem:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3659c246-0bec-490d-8e2d-84d691bf4b8e)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.5 Zonation, succession and change in ecosystems > 2.5A Zonation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5A Zonation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify distinct zones in a pond ecosystem diagram and describe how an abiotic factor changes across them—this is classic ecosystem zonation (e.g., littoral/limnetic/profundal/benthic) and associated abiotic gradients like light, oxygen, or temperature.

parts.content: Identify two distinct zones visible in the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correctly identified zone, up to <marks>2 marks</marks>:

- Littoral zone
- Limnetic zone 
- Profundal zone
- Benthic zone

parts.content: Explain how one abiotic factor changes across these zones.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct explanation, up to <marks>2 marks</marks>:

- Light availability decreases with depth
- Temperature stratification occurs in deeper areas
- Oxygen levels decrease in deeper zones

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.5 Zonation, succession and change in ecosystems > 2.5B Succession
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5B Succession
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for a description of the process of primary succession and pioneer species, which are core components of the general succession topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.5 Zonation, succession and change in ecosystems > 2.5E Climax communities and plagioclimax (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5E Climax communities and plagioclimax (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly mentions describing the climax community, which is a specific focus area under this leaf topic.

parts.content: Describe the process of primary succession, including pioneer species and climax community.
parts.markscheme:
- Pioneer species (such as lichens or mosses) are the first to colonize areas with no pre-existing ecosystem (e.g., bare rock, new lava flow) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gradual changes in species composition occur over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Process leads to a relatively stable climax community <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soil development occurs throughout the process <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.5 Zonation, succession and change in ecosystems > 2.5B Succession
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5B Succession
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question directly asks for a description of the process of secondary succession and a comparison between primary and secondary succession, which falls explicitly under the 'Succession' topic.

parts.content: Describe the process of secondary succession and how it differs from primary succession.
parts.markscheme:
- Secondary succession occurs in areas where an ecosystem previously existed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It starts with existing soil and often a seed bank <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Generally progresses faster than primary succession <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May not go through the same stages as primary succession <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can be initiated by disturbances like fire, flooding, or human activities <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Table 1:** Soil characteristics and dominant vegetation recorded at various stages of primary succession following the retreat of a glacier in Alaska.

| Years since glacial retreat | Soil Nitrogen content ($g \cdot m^{-2}$) | Average Soil Depth ($cm$) | Dominant Vegetation Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 10 | 1.2 | 0.5 | Mosses and Lichens |
| 60 | 14.8 | 9.2 | Alder shrubs |
| 200 | 42.5 | 28.0 | Sitka Spruce forest |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5B Succession

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5E Climax communities and plagioclimax (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) explicitly asks about progression toward a climax community, linking successional stages to climax community development.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1F Soil formation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1F Soil formation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Table focuses on increasing soil depth and nitrogen content over time, reflecting soil development/formation during primary succession.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1G Soil properties (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1G Soil properties (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Abiotic changes referenced include soil nitrogen content and soil depth—key soil properties affecting vegetation establishment.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.2 Energy and biomass in ecosystems > 2.2F Food Web and Biomass
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.2F Food Web and Biomass
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for a field method to estimate total dry-weight biomass of ground-level vegetation, which falls under biomass measurement in ecosystems.

parts.content: Explain how **two** changes in the abiotic environment shown in Table 1 facilitate the progression toward a climax community.
parts.markscheme:
### Change 1: Increase in Soil Nitrogen
- Pioneer species and early colonizers (like Alder) often have symbiotic nitrogen-fixing bacteria in their roots. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As these plants die and decompose, they increase the nitrogen content of the soil, making it fertile enough to support larger, more nutrient-demanding species such as Spruce trees. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Change 2: Increase in Soil Depth
- The accumulation of organic matter from decaying vegetation, combined with the biological weathering of the underlying rock by roots and lichens, increases the soil depth over time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Deeper soil provides the structural support and anchorage necessary for the complex root systems of large trees, while also increasing the volume of the soil reservoir for water and nutrient storage. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the procedure used to estimate the total dry-weight biomass of the ground-level vegetation in the 60-year-old successional stage.
parts.markscheme:
- Use a random sampling technique (e.g., a random number generator for coordinates) to place several quadrats of a known area (e.g., $1 m^2$) within the study site. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Harvest all above-ground plant material (e.g., grasses, small shrubs) within each quadrat and remove any non-living debris or soil. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Place the collected vegetation in a drying oven at a low temperature (approximately $60-70^{\circ}C$) and weigh it periodically until the mass remains constant, indicating all water has been removed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- The distribution of organisms in distinct zones based on environmental factors $\Rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Influenced by factors such as water depth, light penetration, and substrate type $\Rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Results in characteristic species assemblages in different zones $\Rightarrow$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.5 Zonation, succession and change in ecosystems > 2.5B Succession
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5B Succession
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The core concept of the question is the ecological process of succession and how its natural progression can be altered or stopped by external forces.

parts.content: Describe two examples of how human activities can disrupt natural succession processes.
parts.markscheme:
- Deforestation: Resets succession and can lead to soil degradation <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Invasive species introduction: Can alter succession pathways and outcompete native species <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Urbanization: Creates barriers to succession and fragments habitats <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Agriculture: Maintains ecosystems in early successional stages through regular disturbance <marks>2 marks</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.4 Climate and biomes > 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to define 'biome' and describe its key characteristics, which directly matches the biomes sub-topic within Climate and biomes.

parts.content: Define the term "biome" and explain its key characteristics.
parts.markscheme:
- A biome is a large-scale ecosystem defined by climate and dominant vegetation types <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characterized by similar plant and animal communities <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Influenced by factors such as temperature, precipitation, and latitude <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.4 Climate and biomes > 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The prompt asks for characteristics of the savanna biome; describing biome features (vegetation structure, fauna, seasonality, climate) falls under the general study of biomes.

parts.content: Describe the characteristics of the savanna biome.
parts.markscheme:
- Tropical grassland with scattered trees <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Distinct wet and dry seasons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dominated by grasses and drought-resistant trees <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports large herbivore populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regular fires play a role in maintaining ecosystem structure <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.4 Climate and biomes > 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for adaptations of plants within the desert biome, which is a core component of the study of biomes in Topic 2.4B.

parts.content: Outline four adaptations of xerophytic plants to survive in desert biomes.
parts.markscheme:
- Succulent tissues (thickened stems/leaves) store water for dry periods; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced leaf surface area (e.g., small leaves/needles/spines) reduces transpiration; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Thick, waxy cuticle and/or sunken stomata reduces water loss; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extensive root systems (deep tap roots and/or widespread shallow roots) increase water uptake from groundwater or brief rainfall events. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how plant adaptations in desert biomes help to maintain primary productivity despite low and unpredictable precipitation.
parts.markscheme:
- Water storage (succulence) maintains photosynthesis and growth during drought periods; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced transpiration (waxy cuticle; reduced leaf area; sunken stomata; stomatal closure) improves water-use efficiency, allowing net photosynthesis to continue at lower water availability; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CAM photosynthesis shifts stomatal opening to night, reducing daytime water loss while still allowing CO$_2$ uptake for photosynthesis; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Root adaptations (deep and/or widespread shallow roots) increase access to limited water (groundwater and brief rainfall), supporting continued uptake for photosynthesis; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phenological/physiological responses (e.g., dormancy/rapid growth after rain; short life cycles in ephemerals) exploit brief wet periods to fix carbon quickly; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Protective structures (spines/hairs) can reduce leaf temperature and boundary-layer water loss, improving photosynthetic efficiency in hot, dry conditions; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links to GPP/NPP: maintaining carbon fixation while limiting water loss sustains GPP, and reduced stress-related respiration can help sustain NPP; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion/value judgement: overall, adaptations increase water-use efficiency so some primary productivity can be maintained, but productivity remains lower and more variable than in wetter biomes due to persistent water limitation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: To what extent are adaptations of desert plants sufficient to ensure long-term ecosystem stability in desert biomes?
parts.markscheme:
- 0 marks: Not relevant or no standard reached.
- 1–3 marks: Limited outline of a few plant adaptations (e.g., waxy cuticle, deep roots) with little linkage to ecosystem stability; assertions about stability with minimal justification; few or no examples; little or no balance.
- 4–6 marks: Some accurate explanation of how adaptations support survival and productivity (e.g., CAM, succulence, reduced transpiration) linked to aspects of stability (resilience/resistance, nutrient cycling, food webs); some balance by recognising limiting factors (e.g., multi-year drought, low soil fertility, slow recovery); at least one relevant example (e.g., cacti, creosote bush); conclusion present but limited support.
- 7–9 marks: Detailed, well-balanced evaluation of the extent to which adaptations promote stability, clearly distinguishing resistance and resilience; integrates multiple ecosystem processes (energy flow, NPP, water and nutrient cycling, species interactions) and acknowledges constraints (e.g., extreme interannual rainfall variability, episodic disturbance, low decomposition rates, soil salinisation, desertification/overgrazing/climate change); uses well-developed, pertinent examples; explicit, supported conclusion judging that adaptations enhance persistence of individual species and contribute to stability, but do not fully ensure long-term ecosystem stability because stability is also controlled by external drivers and human impacts.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.5B Succession

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Fire can affect resilience (recovery rate/ability to return) and may shift ecosystems to alternative stable states, which links to ecosystem resilience within succession and change.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which fire can influence ecosystem succession.
parts.markscheme:
- Resets succession by clearing existing vegetation <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Releases nutrients stored in biomass, temporarily increasing soil fertility <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Stimulates germination of fire-adapted species <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Creates habitat heterogeneity, potentially increasing biodiversity <marks>2 marks</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2F Resilience of a system

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires knowledge of the soil system, specifically identifying transfers and transformations within it.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) requires understanding of the basic components of a system (storages and matter) applied to an aquatic context.

parts.content: Identify **two** major storages of matter that are present in a named aquatic ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Biomass (e.g., fish, plankton, aquatic plants) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved nutrients (e.g., nitrates, phosphates in the water column) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Benthic sediments (e.g., organic matter or minerals on the lake/sea floor) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved gases (e.g., oxygen, carbon dioxide) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The water body itself (as a storage of $$H_2O$$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid storage identified, up to 2 marks. Do not credit flows (e.g., precipitation or runoff) unless they are clearly defined as a storage within the context of the system.</note>

parts.content: State **one** transfer and **one** transformation of matter that occur within a soil system.
parts.markscheme:
- Transfer: Leaching (minerals moving downwards through soil profiles) / Runoff / Infiltration of water / Deposition of leaf litter <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transformation: Decomposition (organic matter turning into inorganic nutrients) / Weathering of parent rock / Nitrification / Denitrification <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks. Ensure one of each (transfer and transformation) is provided for full marks.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of models (such as systems diagrams or computer simulations) in understanding the complexities of an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Models simplify complex systems, making them easier to visualize and study <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They allow scientists to predict future changes or the impacts of human activities (e.g., climate change models) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They serve as effective communication tools for explaining environmental issues to the public or policy-makers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They can identify gaps in current knowledge or data by highlighting where the model fails to match observations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, models are often over-simplified and may miss emergent properties or small-scale interactions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The accuracy of a model is limited by the quality of the input data ("garbage in, garbage out") <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models often cannot account for unpredictable, stochastic events such as natural disasters <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Predictions are often probabilistic and involve a high degree of uncertainty, which can be misinterpreted <marks>1 mark</marks>



<note>Accept any other reasonable evaluation points. </note>

parts.content:
Environmental systems are often characterized by their resilience and state of equilibrium. 

Discuss the role of feedback mechanisms in determining whether a system maintains a stable equilibrium or crosses a tipping point into a new state.

- Tree line marks the altitude limit for tree growth <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Table: Abiotic and biotic changes observed along a sand dune transect (psammosere) from the shoreline inland.**

| Successional Stage | Distance from Shore (m) | Soil pH | Organic Matter (%) | Plant Species Richness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Embryo Dune | 15 | 8.4 | 0.2 | 3 |
| Yellow Dune | 60 | 8.1 | 0.8 | 12 |
| Grey Dune | 180 | 6.8 | 4.5 | 24 |
| Scrubland | 350 | 5.8 | 8.2 | 18 |
| Mature Woodland | 600 | 5.2 | 14.6 | 15 |

parts.content: State **two** abiotic factors, other than soil pH, that would be expected to change as the distance from the shore increases.
Distinguish between the terms **primary succession** and **secondary succession**.
Explain how pioneer species in the "Yellow Dune" stage (such as Marram grass) facilitate the colonisation of the site by later successional species.
Describe why the Net Community Productivity ($$NCP$$) of the "Mature Woodland" climax community is lower than that of the "Grey Dune" intermediate stage.

parts.content: Outline the influence of altitude on the distribution and characteristics of mountain biomes.
parts.markscheme:
- As altitude increases, the temperature decreases (lapse rate), which mimics the change in temperature seen when moving from the equator towards the poles, creating distinct vertical zonation of vegetation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased precipitation on windward slopes (orographic lifting) supports denser forest biomes, while the leeward rain shadow creates more arid, scrub-like conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher altitudes are subjected to greater wind speeds and increased UV radiation, favoring specialized, low-growing plants/stunted trees (Krummholz effect) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soil depth and nutrient availability typically decrease with elevation due to erosion and slower decomposition rates, restricting primary productivity at higher altitudes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how industrial emissions leading to acid deposition can impact the productivity and nutrient cycling of temperate forest biomes.
parts.markscheme:
### Impacts of acid deposition on temperate forest productivity

- Combustion of fossil fuels (e.g., in power plants or vehicles) releases sulfur dioxide ($SO_2$) and nitrogen oxides ($NO_x$) into the atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These gases react with atmospheric water vapor to form sulfuric and nitric acids, which fall as wet or dry deposition <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acidification of soil causes the leaching of essential plant nutrients (such as calcium, potassium, and magnesium), depriving trees of the minerals needed for growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The low pH mobilizes toxic aluminum ions in the soil, which damage fine root hairs and inhibit the uptake of water and nutrients <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Direct contact with acidic mist or rain can damage the protective waxy cuticle of leaves, reducing photosynthetic efficiency and making trees more vulnerable to disease/pests <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disruption of soil decomposers (fungi and bacteria) by high acidity slows the rate of nutrient cycling, further reducing the availability of nutrients for primary producers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Combined, these factors lead to reduced biomass production (NPP) and may eventually cause forest dieback/decline <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: To what extent does the environmental value system (EVS) of a society determine the success of its biodiversity conservation strategies?

parts.markscheme:

- Low species diversity compared to temperate forests <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Underlain by permafrost in some areas <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the relationship between the second law of thermodynamics and the increase in entropy within an ecological pyramid of energy.
parts.markscheme:
### Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy
- The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy (disorder) of a system increases over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In an ecological pyramid, energy is transformed from one trophic level to the next (e.g., from primary producers to herbivores)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These transformations are never $100\%$ efficient; a significant portion of energy is lost as low-quality heat to the surroundings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This loss of heat represents an increase in entropy, meaning energy is degraded and becomes unavailable to do work for the next trophic level  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare and contrast static equilibrium and steady-state equilibrium, providing a named example for each type.
parts.markscheme:
### Similarities
- Both represent states of balance within a system  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In both states, the overall system properties (observable state) appear to remain constant over a period of time <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Differences
- **Steady-state equilibrium:** Involves constant inputs and outputs of energy and matter, but the system remains in a constant state (e.g., a mature forest ecosystem or a living organism)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Static equilibrium:** No inputs or outputs of matter or energy occur, and there is no change in the system over time (e.g., a pile of rocks or a chair) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state equilibrium is characteristic of open systems, whereas static equilibrium is generally associated with non-living/isolated components  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state systems can return to equilibrium after a small disturbance through negative feedback, while a static system, once moved, establishes a new position  <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Steady-state example: A pond (where water/nutrients enter and leave, but the pond remains the same)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Static example: A building or a geological formation (where no significant energy/matter throughput is required to maintain its structure)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [4] max if no examples are provided or if only similarities/differences are discussed. For comparisons, ensure both sides are mentioned to earn the mark.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how positive feedback mechanisms can drive an ecosystem toward a tipping point, and evaluate the consequences using a named case study.
parts.markscheme:
### Content Guidance
Answers may include:

**Concepts of Feedback and Tipping Points:**
- Positive feedback loops are destabilizing; they amplify changes and drive a system away from its original equilibrium.
- A tipping point is a critical threshold where a small change can push a system into a completely new state/equilibrium.
- Once a tipping point is crossed, the change is often self-sustaining and difficult/impossible to reverse.

**Case Study: Arctic Ice-Albedo Effect:**
- **Mechanism:** Increased global temperatures melt sea ice $\implies$ darker ocean water is exposed $\implies$ albedo (reflectivity) decreases $\implies$ more solar radiation is absorbed $\implies$ temperatures rise further, melting more ice.
- **Consequences:** Loss of habitat for polar species (e.g., polar bears), alteration of global ocean currents (thermohaline circulation), and accelerated sea-level rise.

**Case Study: Eutrophication in a Lake:**
- **Mechanism:** Nutrient runoff leads to algal blooms $\implies$ algae die and decompose $\implies$ oxygen levels drop $\implies$ fish die $\implies$ more decomposition occurs, further stripping oxygen and releasing more nutrients from sediment.
- **Consequences:** Shift from a clear-water, biodiversity-rich state to a turbid, anaerobic, low-diversity state.

**Evaluation:**
- Discussion of the difficulty in predicting exact tipping points due to complex interactions.
- Analysis of the time lags involved in feedback loops.
- Consideration of the socio-economic impacts resulting from the shift to a new ecosystem state.

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| 1–3 | Minimal evidence of understanding positive feedback or tipping points. Fragmented statements with little or no link to a specific case study. |
| 4–6 | Sound understanding of feedback mechanisms. Includes a relevant case study with clear identification of the feedback loop and the resulting shift in equilibrium. Some analysis of consequences provided. |
| 7–9 | Substantial understanding of the relationship between positive feedback and tipping points. Effective use of a detailed case study. Thorough evaluation of the ecological and/or social consequences. Explicit judgments regarding the stability and irreversibility of the system. |

parts.content: Explain the role of abiotic limiting factors in determining the global distribution of terrestrial biomes.
parts.markscheme:
- **Insolation/Solar radiation**: Varies with latitude; higher intensity at the equator drives higher temperatures and rates of photosynthesis/primary productivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Temperature**: Determines the length of the growing season and metabolic rates of organisms; extreme cold (tundra) or heat (deserts) limits species diversity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Precipitation**: Total annual rainfall and its seasonal distribution (e.g., tropical rainforest vs. savanna) determine the available moisture for plant growth. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **P/E Ratio (Precipitation to Evaporation)**: If P/E is >1, water is plentiful (forests); if P/E is <1, evaporation exceeds rainfall, leading to water stress (deserts/grasslands). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not credit biotic factors like competition or predation, as the question specifically asks for abiotic limiting factors in the context of biome distribution.</note>

parts.content: Distinguish between the processes of zonation and succession, using named examples to illustrate your answer.
parts.markscheme:
### Differences between Zonation and Succession

- **Definition of Zonation**: Spatial change in the community structure along an environmental gradient. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Definition of Succession**: Temporal change in the community structure over time at a single location. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Driving Factors**: 
  - Zonation is primarily driven by abiotic gradients (e.g., altitude, salinity, tidal exposure). <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Succession is driven by biotic interactions and the modification of the environment by the organisms themselves (e.g., soil formation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Example of Zonation**: e.g., Different seaweed species arranged in horizontal bands on a rocky shore due to varying tolerance to desiccation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Example of Succession**: e.g., The progression from bare rock to lichen, then moss, grasses, shrubs, and finally oak forest (lithosere). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Reversibility/Stability**: Zonation is usually a permanent feature of the landscape as long as the gradient exists, while succession moves toward a dynamic equilibrium (climax community). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Students must clearly state which process is spatial and which is temporal to gain full marks for the distinction.</examTip>

parts.content: Evaluate the changes in ecosystem energetics and diversity that occur as a community develops from a pioneer stage to a climax community.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include_:

*   **Gross Primary Productivity (GPP)**: Low in pioneer stages due to low leaf area/biomass; increases significantly through intermediate stages as vegetation cover increases; may stabilize or slightly decline in the climax stage as the system reaches maturity.
*   **Net Primary Productivity (NPP)**: High in early/intermediate stages as plants invest energy in rapid growth; in the climax community, NPP approaches zero because GPP is balanced by high community respiration ($R$) ($P/R$ ratio approaches 1).
*   **Biomass**: Increases steadily throughout succession, reaching its maximum at the climax stage where complex vertical stratification (e.g., canopy layers) is present.
*   **Diversity**: 
    - **Species richness**: Usually increases through the seral stages.
    - **Diversity indices**: Low in pioneer stages (few hardy species); peaks in late-intermediate stages; may slightly decrease in the final climax stage as dominant species outcompete others.
*   **Nutrient Cycling**: Pioneer stages have open cycles with high nutrient loss; climax communities have closed, efficient cycles with nutrients stored primarily in the biomass rather than the soil.
*   **Food Web Complexity**: Transitions from simple linear food chains (pioneer) to complex, branched food webs with many trophic levels and specialized niches (climax), leading to increased stability.
*   **Conclusion**: Succession represents a shift from $r$-strategists (pioneers) to $K$-strategists (climax), where the ecosystem moves from a state of high energy accumulation to a state of high maintenance and complexity.

<note>Award 7-9 marks if the answer provides a balanced evaluation of both energetics (GPP, NPP, R) and diversity/complexity with a clear progression through seral stages.</note>

parts.content: Identify **two** transfers and **two** transformations of energy or matter that occur within a glacier system.
parts.markscheme:
Potential transfers (any two): 
- Movement of ice mass downslope due to gravity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accumulation of snow/precipitation on the glacier surface. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Movement of meltwater through internal channels or out of the snout. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Wind-blown transport of surface debris. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Potential transformations (any two):
- Solid ice melting into liquid water. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Snow compressing and recrystallizing into dense glacial ice (firnification). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sublimation of ice directly into water vapor. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Absorption of solar radiation being converted into heat/internal energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each correctly identified process, up to a maximum of 2 for transfers and 2 for transformations.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of a box-and-arrow flow diagram (a model) to represent the storages and flows of a named ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
**Strengths:**
- Simplifies complex biological/geochemical interactions into a format that is easy to visualize. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows for the quantitative comparison of different storages (e.g., biomass vs. litter in a rainforest). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps to identify the most significant pathways of energy or matter loss. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can be used to predict how a change in one component (e.g., removal of a predator) might affect the rest of the system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Limitations:**
- Often oversimplifies the system by excluding minor species or pathways that may still be critical. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Static diagrams do not easily show how flows change over time or with seasons. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The accuracy of the model is entirely dependent on the quality of the initial data collected. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It may ignore the 'human factor' or external unpredictable disturbances (e.g., sudden forest fires). <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Application to named ecosystem:**
- For example, in a Tropical Rainforest: Storages (Biomass, Litter, Soil) and Flows (Leaching, Decomposition, Uptake). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>A maximum of 4 marks may be awarded for strengths and a maximum of 4 marks for limitations. A named ecosystem must be referenced for the highest marks.</note>

parts.content:
"The stability of an environmental system is solely dependent on the presence of negative feedback loops."

Discuss the validity of this statement with reference to named examples of both positive and negative feedback.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:
- Definition of stability/equilibrium (steady-state vs. static).
- Explanation of negative feedback: mechanisms that reverse/dampen a change, bringing the system back to its original state (e.g., predator-prey cycles, temperature regulation in organisms).
- Explanation of positive feedback: mechanisms that amplify change, driving the system toward a tipping point or a new equilibrium (e.g., Albedo effect in the Arctic, methane release from thawing permafrost).
- Discussion on resilience: high biodiversity and complex webs contribute to stability alongside feedback.
- Discussion on how positive feedback can lead to system collapse or a "regime shift" (loss of current stability).

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for a balanced analysis of the extent to which negative feedback is the "sole" factor, acknowledging the role of other factors like ecosystem complexity or the destabilizing influence of positive feedback.

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains: • minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

parts.content: Outline **two** ecosystem services in a named biome.
parts.markscheme:
**Tundra**

- Permafrost/glaciers in tundra provide important storage in hydrological cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ice in tundra provides reflective surface/increases planetary albedo, thus moderating global temperatures <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Wetlands**

- Decomposers/high productivity in swamps/wetlands provides filtration of inorganic nutrients / water purification <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Storage of water in wetlands prevents flooding / provides ideal resting grounds for migratory birds <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Tropical rainforests

- High biodiversity in tropical rainforests promotes ecotourism/recreation <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- High rate of photosynthesis in tropical rainforests maintains balance of O2/CO2 in atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Boreal/temperate forests

- Tree populations in boreal/temperate forests prevent soil erosion on mountainsides <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Forest canopies in forests provide shade/shelter/habitat for diversity of species <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Be careful only to credit "services" (maintenance/establishment of favourable conditions) and not "goods" (consumable/harvestable/physical products).</note>  

<note>Award **[2 max]** if no biome is identified. If more than one biome is given, credit only highest scoring biome addressed.</note>

parts.content: Explain the causes, and the possible consequences, of the loss of a named critically endangered species.
parts.markscheme:
### Causes <marks>3 max</marks>

- Habitat loss / deforestation
- Habitat degradation / pollution
- Narrowly distributed / endemic
- Poaching / overhunting
- Illegal trafficking of species
- Disease
- Small population size/gene pool / inbreeding
- Specialised niche
- Slow reproduction rate / specialised reproductive behaviour
- High trophic level/top predator
- Low/negative cultural value
- Influence/competition/predation from invasive species

### Consequences <marks>3 max</marks>

- Loss of an aesthetically attractive organism
- Loss of ethically significant life / breach of biorights
- Loss of biodiversity
- Increase in organisms upon which the species fed or competed with
- Decline in other organisms due to loss of food source
- If keystone species, widespread impacts/cascade effects on food chains/ecosystem
- Economic costs from loss of ecosystem services provided by the species
- Economic costs from loss of tourism opportunities
- Social impacts on local culture as important/significant cultural loss

<note>Max. 4 marks without explaination </note>

parts.content: Using examples, discuss whether habitat conservation is more successful than a species-based approach to protecting threatened species.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 
- Habitat conservation aims to protect entire ecosystems and the various species within them, while a species-based approach focuses on specific threatened species.
- Protects multiple species and their interactions within an ecosystem 
- Addresses root causes of biodiversity loss like habitat destruction and fragmentation 
- Maintains ecosystem services like water purification, nutrient cycling, and climate regulation 
- Examples: Establishing protected areas, restoring degraded habitats, sustainable forestry practices 
- Targets critically endangered species at high risk of extinction 
- Allows for focused efforts on specific species' needs and threats 
- Examples: Captive breeding programs, reintroduction efforts, anti-poaching measures

| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

parts.content: Soil development
parts.markscheme:
- As succession progresses, weathering of rock and accumulation of organic matter increase soil depth and nutrient availability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Improved soil structure and fertility allow larger and more complex plant species to establish, accelerating succession. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Species interactions
parts.markscheme:
- Interactions such as competition can lead to some species outcompeting others, causing changes in species composition over time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitation (e.g. pioneer species modifying the environment) or mutualism can make conditions more suitable for later successional species. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Primary succession was studied on a volcanic island following a major eruption. Ecologists measured various abiotic and biotic factors across three distinct successional stages. The results of the survey are presented in Table 1.

**Table 1: Environmental factors and species abundance across three successional stages**

| Feature / Species Observed                  | Colonization | Early Seral | Late Seral |
|---------------------------------------------|--------------|-------------|------------|
| Mean soil depth (cm)                        | 0.1          | 3.5         | 18.0       |
| Lichen (*Lecidella*)                        | 120          | 45          | 10         |
| Moss (*Racomitrium*)                        | 15           | 90          | 30         |
| Arctic willow (*Salix arctica*)             | 0            | 25          | 50         |
| Dwarf birch (*Betula nana*)                 | 0            | 5           | 35         |
| **Total plant species richness**            | 2            | 12          | 28         |
| **Simpson’s diversity index (D)**           | 1.28         | 2.45        | ---        |


**Figure 1: Pioneer organisms colonizing bare basalt rock**

![Pioneer Community](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-ef9f10f7-3c4d-4741-aa76-be133cc4641a-1771929956947/a25cbad5-fa6a-441f-997f-02d65d7ebf5c.jpeg)

parts.content: Explain the relationship between soil development and the changes in species richness during this process.
State **one** method to estimate the abundance of the moss species shown in Figure 1.

questions.specification:
**Figure 1: Taiga**

*   Taiga is the world’s largest terrestrial biome.
*   The Siberian taiga contains about 20 % of the world’s forested areas.
*   The taiga includes evergreen trees such as pine, spruce, fir, and some deciduous trees such as birch and poplar.
*   Nutrient-poor soil and the climatic conditions contribute to slow rate of tree growth.
*   Threats to taiga include forest fires, clearance for agriculture land, logging for timber, mining, road building and climate change.
*   The region suffers deforestation rates of 20000km2 annually.
*   Conservation measures include restoration of forests through replanting and designating protected areas such as National Parks and selective logging policies.
*   There is high global demand for timber with markets throughout Asia, Europe and the USA. Economic incentives to increase exports have increased timber production, destroying more of the forest ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 7: Natural resources > 7.1 Natural resources - uses and management > 7.1B Ecosystem services
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 7.1B Ecosystem services
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for an ecological service provided by the taiga, which directly targets ecosystem services.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.3 Biogeochemical cycles > 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Deforestation affecting the world’s oceans is most plausibly via increased atmospheric CO2/reduced carbon sequestration, linking terrestrial forests to the carbon cycle and ocean uptake.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly connects land deforestation to impacts on the oceans, commonly explained through altered CO2 levels and oceans’ role as a carbon sink (ocean acidification).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 3: Conservation and biodiversity > 3.3 Conservation and regeneration > 3.3B Regeneration and rewilding
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.3B Regeneration and rewilding
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) concerns restoration through replanting and what limits regeneration, fitting regeneration/restoration concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.4 Climate and biomes > 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The stimulus and questions are set in the taiga biome context (climate/soil constraints), so understanding biomes underpins limiting factors and services.

parts.content: Outline **one** ecological service provided by the Siberian taiga.
Explain how deforestation in the taiga may impact the world’s oceans.
State **one** natural limiting factor to the successful restoration of taiga through replanting.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following (<marks>1 max</marks>):

nutrient poor soils / low temperature / low rainfall / limited growing season / limited amount of sunlight / slow growth rates / permafrost inhibits plant growth;

_Do not accept ‘wildfires / grazers/animals eat the plants/seedlings’._  
_Do not accept just ‘temperature/rainfall/soil fertility/time it takes for trees to grow/long mature time for trees’._
